index.html.title: index
index.html.description:
Experiments with random walks on graphs and the correspondence between walk statistics and physical notions.Consider a graph where we perform a random walk of fixed length from node to node . At each intermediate step, the distribution of the particle's position defines a "trajectory." On an infinite lattice, this trajectory corresponds to uniform motion. By adding position-dependent termination probabilities (potential energy), the trajectory shows the particle slowing in high-potential regions.hammy explores this correspondence between walk statistics and physics: Continuous position on a discrete graph (via NNLS spectral decomposition) Lagrangian and momentum (future work) Hamiltonian function (future work) Experiment 1: One-Dimensional Walk — develop the position algorithm, validate error metrics, establish how many simulations are needed
Architecture and Pipeline — dual-language simulation, caching, cloud execution
Cost Analysis — throughput and cost per data point across GPU/CPU configurations
Theoretical notes and hypotheses in Ideas.

ideas/is_hamiltonian_fermionic.html.title: is_hamiltonian_fermionic
ideas/is_hamiltonian_fermionic.html.description:
#hamiltonian #cellural_automata #antisymmetry #fock_space
In Wikipedia article about Fock Space there is that it is a direct sum of antisymmetric tensors of single particles. In Hamiltonian mechanics, tensor is also antisymmetric. Maybe that means that Hamiltonian motion can be represented as a cellural automate where two particles cannot be located in the same cell.

Bra-ket notation is conditional operation
Complex amplitudes OR probabilities need to be done if we take square roots of steps
Momentum space is just the space of different target points after N steps

experiments/1_walk.html.title: 1_walk
experiments/1_walk.html.description:
Consider a random walk on a one-dimensional lattice of length steps. At each step the particle moves with equal probability. We condition on the walk ending at a particular position from the set . For each target, we record the distribution of the particle's position at intermediate checkpoints .The raw data is a histogram counting how many walks with target had position at checkpoint .Develop a trajectory function that: Maps a distribution over graph nodes to a single position on the graph, Does not depend on the labelling of the graph nodes (topology-agnostic), Gives a continuous-valued position even though the graph is discrete, Provides quantifiable uncertainty. The naive approach (empirical mode or weighted mean) requires node coordinates, which don't exist on a general graph. This experiment develops and validates a coordinate-free approach.The simulation ran on a Yandex Cloud V100 GPU, producing data points across 5 levels (16 minutes of compute time). Three platforms (PYTHON, CFFI, CUDA) ran in parallel, producing independent datasets that are compared for correctness and then aggregated.The raw histograms at selected checkpoints (aggregated across all platforms and levels):Each column is a checkpoint (100, 300, 500, 700, 900 steps); each row is a target (0, 1, 2, 5, 10). The distributions shift linearly toward the target as the checkpoint increases, consistent with the conditioned walk drifting toward its endpoint.The transition matrix of the lazy walk (with , ) has eigendecomposition . The column gives the distribution after steps starting at node .Given observed distribution (normalized to a probability vector), we find the walk power by matching its spectral spread to the mean spectral spread of columns:where are spectral coefficients, are eigenvalues, and are rows of . The right-hand side is monotonically decreasing in , giving a unique root via bisection.At the optimal , compute via spectral decomposition and solve the non-negative least squares problem:The solution decomposes as a non-negative mixture of point-source diffusions. Components with weight of the maximum are discarded.The surviving NNLS components (graph nodes with positive weights) are mapped to a graph element: 1 node: position is on a vertex (0-simplex) 2 adjacent nodes: position is on an edge (1-simplex), weights are barycentric coordinates mutually adjacent nodes: position is on a -simplex A greedy algorithm builds the largest valid simplex: start with the highest-weight node, add the next-highest only if it is adjacent to all nodes already in the simplex.Continuous position is computed as the weighted sum of simplex node indices using barycentric coordinates: .The continuous position (blue line) tracks the theoretical trajectory (black line) for all targets. The light-blue band shows the bootstrap 95% CI:
Each column is a platform (CFFI, CUDA, PYTHON, TOTAL); each row is a target. Key observations: For targets , the position follows the theoretical line with sub-node precision. For , the position stays at node 50 (the starting point). The PYTHON column shows a wide CI because the PYTHON platform produces the fewest data points (~6M vs ~270B for CUDA). All platforms agree on the position, confirming simulation correctness. The position at checkpoint 500 converges rapidly across simulation levels (each level doubles the data):
For CFFI, CUDA, and TOTAL, the position is stable from level 0. The PYTHON platform converges by level 2-3 due to fewer samples.At checkpoint 500, level 4, TOTAL platform:Integer targets (0, 2, 10) land exactly on vertices; half-integer targets (1, 5 mapped to 50.5, 52.5) land on edges with barycentric coordinates .We considered three metrics. Only two proved useful.Resample the histogram via multinomial with resamples. For each resample, run the full position pipeline and collect . The standard deviation of gives the position uncertainty without assuming a true position.
The bootstrap std decreases with simulation level (more data) as expected. The PYTHON column shows the largest uncertainty (fewest samples), decreasing from ~0.1 at level 0 to ~0.01 at level 4. CFFI and CUDA are near zero at all levels due to their much higher throughput. For target 0, the position is exactly on a vertex (node 50) across all resamples, giving zero std.The fraction of total NNLS weight captured by the identified simplex: .
In this 1D experiment everywhere — all weight maps to an edge or vertex. The greedy simplex identification never fails on this graph. On higher-dimensional graphs, would signal that the distribution doesn't decompose cleanly into a point on a simplex.The relative residual :
The residual converges to , not to zero. This is model error, not sampling noise: the conditioned walk distribution is not exactly representable as a non-negative mixture of columns. The non-negativity constraint in NNLS prevents the exact decomposition because conditioning on the endpoint creates a distribution shape that requires "subtracting" some columns. Since the residual is constant regardless of data quality, it does not distinguish well-localized from poorly-localized positions. It can serve as a sanity check — anomalous values would signal bugs. The position algorithm generalizes to any graph with a transition matrix. No changes needed for 2D/3D lattices except adjusting max_simplex_dim (1 for edges, 2 for faces). Bootstrap is the primary error metric. Simplex fit quality becomes important on higher-dimensional graphs where simplex identification may fail. At level 4, position is determined to nodes for non-zero targets. For experiments where sub-node precision is not needed, level 2 (4 minutes of GPU time) is sufficient. The number of simulations needed depends on the target: undirected walks () have maximal positional entropy and require proportionally more data. However, on this 1D graph even the PYTHON platform (~6M data points) gives exact vertex positions for symmetric targets — the main uncertainty comes from edge positions where the walker is between two nodes.

headers.heading: Simplex fit quality (useful for higher dimensions)
NNLS residual (not useful for error estimation)

computation_framework/index.html.title: index
computation_framework/index.html.description:
hammy runs Monte Carlo random walk simulations across three platforms in parallel:Each platform produces an independent dataset. Results are compared to detect bugs, then aggregated for final analysis.Each experiment is written once in C using a macro layer (cuda_cpu.h) that compiles to both CPU and GPU:
CPU mode: CUDA's warp (32 threads) is emulated as a sequential loop. __shared__ becomes a local variable, __syncthreads() restarts the loop.
GPU mode: Real warps, real shared memory, real atomic operations. Same source file, different #defines.
The Python implementation serves as a reference. If all three platforms agree on results (checked via the population size metric ), the simulation is correct.Objects form a dependency chain, each caching its results as content-addressed files:Experiment → MachineConfiguration → ExperimentConfiguration → SequentialCalibration → ParallelCalibration → Simulation → Calculation → Visualization
Each object has a deterministic ID derived from its metadata (including all upstream dependencies). If a cached file with that ID exists locally or on S3, it is loaded instead of recomputed. This enables:
Level resumption: --level 4 after a previous --level 2 downloads levels 0-2 from S3
Cross-machine reuse: calibration and experiment configs are cached per machine hash
Incremental development: changing a calculation doesn't invalidate the simulation cache
Each level runs for minutes (level 0 = 1 min, level 4 = 8 min). Results accumulate: level contains the sum of all data from levels . Total simulation time for level : minutes.Experiments run on Yandex Cloud preemptible VMs via Docker containers. The VM self-destructs on success; on failure it stays alive for 5 minutes for SSH debugging.# GPU (V100, 8 cores, 48G RAM)
./create_hammy_machine.sh 5.1 "--level 4 --no-calculations" # CPU only (2 cores, 4G RAM) — for testing
./create_hammy_machine.sh 5.1 "--level 0 --no-calculations" --cpu
S3 credentials are read from ~/secrets.env and injected as container environment variables.pip download --dest docker/wheels --python-version 3.10 --only-binary=:all: \ --platform manylinux2014_x86_64 --platform manylinux_2_17_x86_64 \ xarray cffi psutil boto3 matplotlib h5netcdf h5py scs
docker build -f docker/hammy.Dockerfile -t sckol/hammy:<version> .
docker push sckol/hammy:<version>
rm -rf docker/wheels
Wheels are downloaded offline because Docker builds have unreliable PyPI connectivity.

computation_framework/cost_analysis.html.title: Cost Analysis
computation_framework/cost_analysis.html.description:
Data from Experiment 1 (1D walk, 101-node graph), level 0 (1 minute of simulation):CUDA dominates by 2-3 orders of magnitude. On the V100 machine, the GPU produces 177x more data points per minute than the same machine's 8 CPU cores running CFFI.Yandex Cloud preemptible VM pricing (ru-central1, approximate):GPU is 3.7x cheaper per data point than the best CPU config (4-core), despite being 36x more expensive per hour.CFFI throughput scales linearly with cores, but cost also scales linearly. The cost per data point is the same for 2c and 4c. However, the 8-core CPU is more expensive per data point because the V100 machine's 8-core CFFI benchmark (877K loops/min) reflects better hardware than a standard-v3 8-core VM. The actual standard-v3 8-core would likely match ~2x the 4-core throughput at 2x the cost — same efficiency.The fixed overhead (boot, image pull, calibration) favors larger machines: a 4-core VM finishes the same work in half the wall-clock time as a 2-core, with the same total cost but less time exposed to preemption risk.For a level-4 run (16 minutes of simulation):GPU overhead is lower (5 min vs 15 min) because GPU VMs boot faster and calibration is cached from previous runs. CPU VMs have longer image pull times (7.6 GB image on limited bandwidth).The simulation kernel throughput is independent of graph size — the walk kernel only needs the transition probabilities at the current node, not the full graph. However:
Position calculation (NNLS on matrix) scales as — this should run locally, not on GPU VMs
Bootstrap ( NNLS) also scales with graph size — precompute and bootstrap only the NNLS step
For a 2D lattice with nodes, NNLS becomes the bottleneck. Consider sparse solvers for future experiments.

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the closer connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make the graph appear more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded
